_purpose: County Landscape Profile for Leflore County, MS. County seat: Greenwood, the self-proclaimed 'Cotton Capital of the World' — site of the largest long-staple cotton market in the United States. Leflore County sits at the eastern edge of the Delta where the alluvial plain meets the loess bluffs, and the Tallahatchie and Yalobusha rivers converge to form the Yazoo. Named for Greenwood Leflore, the last principal chief of the Choctaw in Mississippi who remained after removal and became a planter.

physical_geography.physiographic_province: Mississippi Alluvial Plain (Yazoo-Mississippi Delta), eastern edge. The county straddles the transition between the flat Delta floor and the loess bluffs to the east. Greenwood sits at the confluence of the Tallahatchie and Yalobusha rivers, where they join to form the Yazoo.
physical_geography.elevation_range: 95–200 ft. The Delta floor in western Leflore County is among the lowest ground in the region; the eastern edge rises sharply onto the loess bluffs, which are not Delta terrain at all.
physical_geography.dominant_terrain: Mixed — flat alluvial Delta in the western two-thirds, transitioning to rolling loess hills in the east. This transition zone gave Greenwood its strategic position: high enough ground for a town, low enough to access Delta agricultural wealth.

soil_types.description: Heavy alluvial clay dominant in the western and central portions of the county — the Delta floor proper.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Prime cotton land. Leflore County's Delta portion produced long-staple cotton of exceptional quality, commanding premium prices on the Greenwood market.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Leflore County, Mississippi (1965).

soil_types.description: Well-drained loess-derived silt loam on the eastern bluffs. Very different from Delta alluvial soils.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Suitable for diversified farming and timber. The bluff soils supported smaller, more diversified farms than the Delta floor — a different agricultural world just a few miles from the cotton monoculture.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Leflore County, Mississippi (1965).

major_waterways.name: Yazoo River (formed at Greenwood)
major_waterways.role: Major navigation, commercial artery, county landmark
major_waterways.description: The Yazoo River is formed at Greenwood by the confluence of the Tallahatchie and Yalobusha rivers. The Yazoo flows south to Vicksburg and the Mississippi. Before railroads, the Yazoo was the Delta's primary commercial highway — cotton bales were loaded on steamboats at Greenwood and shipped south to Vicksburg and New Orleans.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: At Greenwood, the Tallahatchie and Yalobusha rivers joined to become the Yazoo — and at that confluence {ancestor_name}'s cotton entered the commercial world, loaded onto steamboats bound for Vicksburg and New Orleans, where it became money.

major_waterways.name: Tallahatchie River
major_waterways.description: Flows southwest through the county to its confluence with the Yalobusha at Greenwood. The Tallahatchie drained northern Leflore and Tallahatchie counties.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Tallahatchie River wound through the northern part of {ancestor_name}'s county, a slow brown river that carried cotton downstream and carried nothing back but the current.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Dense bottomland hardwood forest on Delta floor; mixed hardwood-pine on loess bluffs
vegetation_eras.description: The Delta portion was impenetrable swamp forest. The loess bluffs on the eastern edge carried mixed hardwoods and longleaf pine. Named for Greenwood Leflore, the Choctaw chief who negotiated the Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek that ceded the Delta to the United States.
vegetation_eras.source: Cobb, The Most Southern Place on Earth (1992).

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Progressive clearing for cotton on Delta floor
vegetation_eras.description: Clearing began at the river frontage and expanded inland. Greenwood grew as a river port and commercial center. By 1900, most of the Delta portion was cleared, though the eastern bluffs retained more forest cover.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Cotton monoculture on Delta floor; mixed use on bluffs
vegetation_eras.description: The Delta portion became continuous cotton fields. Greenwood's cotton market processed hundreds of thousands of bales annually. The eastern bluffs maintained more diversified agriculture — corn, livestock, timber — creating a visible economic boundary within the county.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: Greenwood in October was the center of the cotton world. The Yazoo River waterfront was stacked with cotton bales five high, waiting for steamboats. Cotton lint floated in the air like snow, settling on every surface — clothes, food, water. The town smelled of cotton seed oil from the crushing mill, river mud from the Yazoo, and the wood smoke of the compression press that squeezed 500-pound bales into steamboat-ready blocks. The cotton exchange hummed with brokers, factors, and planters calculating prices. Away from town, the Sharkey clay fields stretched empty and brown, the harvest stripped clean, stubble standing in rows that ran to the horizon.
sensory_snapshots.source: Cobb, The Most Southern Place on Earth (1992).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: Winter in Leflore County was the quiet season — the cotton was sold, the accounts were settled (always in the planter's favor), and the Sharkey clay turned to a gumbo of mud that made the dirt roads impassable for days after a rain. The tenant cabins along the back roads were cold — single-wall cypress construction with newspaper insulation, heated by a single fireplace or wood stove that kept one room warm while the rest of the cabin froze. The sky was low and gray. The Yazoo ran high and brown with winter rain. In Greenwood, the cotton warehouses stood empty until next season, and the commissary stores extended credit that would not be repaid until the next harvest — if then.
sensory_snapshots.source: Cobb, The Most Southern Place on Earth (1992); Daniel, Breaking the Land (1985).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: Greenwood in the summer of 1962 became a battleground. SNCC workers — Bob Moses, Sam Block, Willie Peacock — set up voter registration operations in the heart of the Delta's most powerful planter county. The physical landscape had not changed — the cotton was growing, the heat was stifling, the roads were still unpaved outside town — but the human landscape was convulsing. White citizens' councils cut off federal surplus food distribution to Black families who attempted to register. Shots were fired into SNCC offices. The sound of the Delta in 1962 was the sound of mass meetings in church basements, freedom songs echoing through the flat fields at night, and the silence of a county where even the act of walking to the courthouse to register could end a person's economic life.
sensory_snapshots.source: Payne, Charles M. I've Got the Light of Freedom: The Organizing Tradition and the Mississippi Freedom Struggle. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1995.

landscape_changes.description: The Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek (1830) ceded Choctaw territory including Leflore County to the United States. The county was named for Greenwood Leflore, the Choctaw chief who negotiated the treaty and controversially remained in Mississippi as a planter rather than removing to Indian Territory. American planters brought enslaved workers to clear the land for cotton.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: The county's very name memorializes the moment of dispossession — Greenwood Leflore surrendered his people's homeland and then became part of the system that exploited it.
landscape_changes.source: Cobb, The Most Southern Place on Earth (1992).

landscape_changes.description: The convergence of river transportation and railroad access made Greenwood the largest long-staple cotton market in the United States. The town's cotton exchange determined prices for the entire Delta region.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} grew cotton in the Delta, the price received was set in Greenwood — a town where the planter class lived in columned houses on the bluff and the sharecropper families who produced the cotton never entered the front door.
landscape_changes.source: Cobb, The Most Southern Place on Earth (1992).

landscape_changes.change: SNCC voter registration and white resistance
landscape_changes.description: Greenwood became a primary target of SNCC's voter registration campaign in 1962-63. The white power structure responded with economic warfare (cutting off food aid), violence (shootings, arson, beatings), and legal harassment. The confrontation in Greenwood was among the most intense of the entire civil rights movement.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: The civil rights movement in Greenwood was not distant news for {ancestor_name} — it was happening at the courthouse, at the church, on the roads between the plantation and the town.
landscape_changes.source: Payne, I've Got the Light of Freedom (1995).

narrative_hooks: Greenwood called itself the Cotton Capital of the World, and {ancestor_name} knew what that title cost — the wealth that built the town's fine houses came from the Delta's Sharkey clay, worked by sharecropper families who saw none of the capital and all of the cotton.

Leflore County took its name from a Choctaw chief who sold his people's land and stayed to become a planter — and that origin story told {ancestor_name} everything about how the Delta worked: someone always profited from someone else's dispossession.

When SNCC organizers arrived in Greenwood in 1962, {ancestor_name}'s community had to make a choice that could cost everything — walk to the courthouse to register, or stay home and keep the fragile safety of silence.

At the confluence of the Tallahatchie and Yalobusha rivers, where the Yazoo began, {ancestor_name}'s cotton entered the market system — loaded onto flatboats and later into railroad cars bound for the gins and warehouses of Greenwood, where other men decided what a year of labor was worth.

The eastern edge of Leflore County rose onto the loess bluffs — and that ten-foot rise in elevation marked the line between the Delta world of cotton monoculture and a different landscape of diversified farms, as if the geology itself had drawn a boundary between two ways of life.
source_references: Cobb, James C. The Most Southern Place on Earth: The Mississippi Delta and the Roots of Regional Identity. New York: Oxford University Press, 1992.
Payne, Charles M. I've Got the Light of Freedom: The Organizing Tradition and the Mississippi Freedom Struggle. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1995.
Willis, John C. Forgotten Time: The Yazoo-Mississippi Delta after the Civil War. Charlottesville: University Press of Virginia, 2000.
Daniel, Pete. Breaking the Land: The Transformation of Cotton, Tobacco, and Rice Cultures since 1880. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1985.
USDA Soil Survey of Leflore County, Mississippi. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1965.
